## Framework Notes

### Vercel AI SDK

motivated the alias table: wrapper spans, missing operation names, attribute-sourced turns.

- Fixture: `test/fixtures/vercel.spans.json`
- Total spans: 4
- Operation counts: invoke_agent:1, chat:2, execute_tool:1
- Known-internal/lifecycle spans intentionally excluded: 0
- Unrecognized spans: 0
- Operation-name patterns matched: /^ai\.generateText\.doGenerate$/ -> chat (2); /^ai\.generateText$/ -> invoke_agent (1); /^ai\.toolCall$/ -> execute_tool (1)
- Attribute aliases fired: gen_ai.request.model <- ai.model.id (1); gen_ai.system <- ai.model.provider (1); gen_ai.usage.input_tokens <- ai.usage.inputTokens (1); gen_ai.usage.output_tokens <- ai.usage.outputTokens (1)
- Tool aliases fired: tool.arguments <- ai.toolCall.args (1); tool.name <- ai.toolCall.name (1); tool.result <- ai.toolCall.result (1)
- Provider normalization: openai.responses -> openai (3)
- Turn extraction: 2/2 chat spans, assistant 1/1 conversation
- Mapper-change difficulty: Highest initial mapper cost: it forced the span-name fallback table, attribute aliases, provider normalization, and Vercel tool aliases.

- Mapper-change difficulty: Moderate incremental cost: no operation fallback, but the bridge exposed parts[].content messages, provider aliasing, and internal lifecycle spans to classify as intentionally excluded.

## Arc

The build difficulty was not raw span count; Mastra has the most spans because it exposes a richer internal lifecycle. The relevant metric is mapper-table/change cost: Vercel forced the general tables into existence, Mastra added standard content-shape/provider/tool-result coverage, OpenAI was the clean baseline, and LangGraph arrived as the proof case with only generic table/parser reuse. Each new framework cost fewer architectural changes than the last; the fourth needed almost nothing. That is the architecture generalizing: four frameworks, one mapper, no framework-specific code path.
